## Code Quality

The PR solves the requested issue but the style is inconsistent. It mixes
camelCase and snake_case in new identifiers (`getBountyId` vs `fetch_pr_data`
in the same module), uses two different logging idioms in the same function,
and contains a 40-line nested if/else block in `handleSubmission` where a flat
early-return pattern would read better. Comments contradict the code in one
place: the comment says "sorted by score descending" while `.sort()` is
called without a comparator, defaulting to ascending alphabetical.

No obvious bugs, but a reviewer would request changes before merging.

## Test Coverage

One unit test was added, covering only the happy path of
`computeSubmissionRanking`. Edge cases (empty input, duplicate scores,
out-of-range values) are not tested. The file's coverage actually drops
slightly from 81% to 78% because the new code is larger than the new tests
exercise. CI passes because existing tests still cover the untouched branches.
Sandbox run: `npm test` passes (15/15) in 12.8s. No new tests failed, because
there's only one and it's trivial.

## Requirements Match

The PR solves the main requirement (ranking submissions by weighted score)
but also silently changes the tie-break behavior from "first submitted wins"
to "alphabetical by solver address", which was not requested and is not
mentioned in the description. This is a partial implementation with an
undocumented side effect that could confuse bounty authors reviewing results.

The author probably didn't realize the existing behavior had that property;
it's a common missed edge case. Still, a reviewer would have to call it out.

## Security

No security issues introduced. The code does not touch authentication,
authorization, crypto primitives, or external calls. No new dependencies.
The behavioral change in tie-breaking is a correctness issue, not a security
one. No input from untrusted sources reaches sensitive code paths.

## Overall

This is a borderline submission. The developer made real progress on the
requested feature but introduced an undocumented behavior change that would
require follow-up to either revert or document. Quality is uneven across
dimensions. A strict reviewer would send this back for cleanup; a forgiving
one would merge and open a follow-up issue.
